fields.brief_block: Tactical and strategic decisions do not constitute ineffective assistance unless no competent attorney would have made the same choice. (Head v. Ferrell, 274 Ga. 399 (2001); Rumph v. State, 307 Ga. 477 (2019)).
fields.opinion_block: [C]ounsel on appeal is "strongly presumed to have rendered adequate assistance and made all significant decisions in the exercise of reasonable professional judgment." . . . Because counsel's performance is considered in light of the circumstances surrounding the representation, reference to hindsight is inappropriate in judging counsel's performance.
fields.finding_analysis: Head v. Ferrell applies the Strickland framework and gives strong deference to counsel's strategic decisions, holding that counsel is strongly presumed to have exercised reasonable professional judgment and that hindsight is inappropriate. That general principle is consistent with the brief's point. But the opinion never articulates the specific standard the brief attributes to it -- that a tactical decision constitutes ineffective assistance only when "no competent attorney would have made the same choice." The case states the deferential presumption and rejects the ineffectiveness claims on the facts; it does not announce the categorical "no competent attorney" test. The proposition is therefore an overstatement of what this opinion actually holds, even though it is in the same doctrinal territory.

fields.brief_block: Strategic decisions, even if ultimately unsuccessful, are entitled to strong deference. (Head v. Ferrell, 274 Ga. 399 (2001); Rumph v. State, 307 Ga. 477 (2019).)
fields.finding_analysis: Head v. Ferrell directly supports this proposition. The opinion holds that counsel is "strongly presumed to have rendered adequate assistance and made all significant decisions in the exercise of reasonable professional judgment" and that "reference to hindsight is inappropriate in judging counsel's performance." In applying that standard, the court repeatedly defers to trial counsel's strategic choices -- for example, the chosen "residual doubt" mitigation theory and the decision not to raise a Batson challenge -- treating them as reasonable professional judgments notwithstanding that the defense was unsuccessful. That is precisely the strong deference to (even unsuccessful) strategic decisions the brief asserts.
